For RLVR you need a binary reward, an example of a successful rollout is not a reward signal. RL needs to upweight successful rollouts over less successful ones, but seeing a successful trace some other model did cannot generate a reward signal on *your models* rollouts. 

You can do RLAIF, but that's a very different paradigm and super inefficient for coding tasks

tweets.text:
The dominant narrative in AI is that superhuman AI hackers are going to be a security nightmare.

I now believe the opposite: everything gets more secure after superhuman LLM hackers, because the commoditization of intelligence favors defenders over attackers.  

Here's why:  

• Before LLMs, defense meant covering every idea that any attacker might come up with. Human minds are diverse and attackers are uncorrelated with each other, so attack ideas had high entropy. N attackers meant N independent darts thrown in idea-space. You could outspend all attackers combined, but if even one gets lucky with and thinks of something you didn't, it's gg. 

• AI collapses that entropy. Attackers and defenders alike now use the same models, which means their ideas are correlated. Even if they're using different models, models are more correlated with each other than humans are. 20 uncoordinated attackers are no longer 20 independent minds: they're running the same search 20 times, burning redundant compute, trying the same attack ideas. 

• So the game reduces to spend. If you assume entropy is literally 0, the defender only needs to spend as much as the best-funded attacker, not the sum of all attackers. IOTW, attackers used to sum, now they max.

• Example: say you have North Korea, Russia, and China all trying to hack you with LLMs, and they're each spending ~$100K of compute trying to attack you ($300K total), you only need to spend ~$200K to ensure nobody gets through. This is because they're not coordinating. In the previous model, they didn't need to coordinate because the entropy in their different attack ideas were large, and thus you'd need to match their collective budgets to stay secure.

This model is obviously a simplification, and it gets compounded by other techniques that favor defenders, like formal verification and better testing.  

But you can already see this playing out in crypto. Big, well-defended protocols have become more secure after superhuman AI has emerged (as I demonstrate in the QT). I believe this is the reason why. It's the same reason why AI slop in writing is so easy to spot, and I think it means the long-term cybersecurity equilibrium is actually a happy one.

tweets.full_text:
The dominant narrative in AI is that superhuman AI hackers are going to be a security nightmare.

I now believe the opposite: everything gets more secure after superhuman LLM hackers, because the commoditization of intelligence favors defenders over attackers.  

Here's why:  

• Before LLMs, defense meant covering every idea that any attacker might come up with. Human minds are diverse and attackers are uncorrelated with each other, so attack ideas had high entropy. N attackers meant N independent darts thrown in idea-space. You could outspend all attackers combined, but if even one gets lucky with and thinks of something you didn't, it's gg. 

• AI collapses that entropy. Attackers and defenders alike now use the same models, which means their ideas are correlated. Even if they're using different models, models are more correlated with each other than humans are. 20 uncoordinated attackers are no longer 20 independent minds: they're running the same search 20 times, burning redundant compute, trying the same attack ideas. 

• So the game reduces to spend. If you assume entropy is literally 0, the defender only needs to spend as much as the best-funded attacker, not the sum of all attackers. IOTW, attackers used to sum, now they max.

• Example: say you have North Korea, Russia, and China all trying to hack you with LLMs, and they're each spending ~$100K of compute trying to attack you ($300K total), you only need to spend ~$200K to ensure nobody gets through. This is because they're not coordinating. In the previous model, they didn't need to coordinate because the entropy in their different attack ideas were large, and thus you'd need to match their collective budgets to stay secure.

This model is obviously a simplification, and it gets compounded by other techniques that favor defenders, like formal verification and better testing.  

But you can already see this playing out in crypto. Big, well-defended protocols have become more secure after superhuman AI has emerged (as I demonstrate in the QT). I believe this is the reason why. It's the same reason why AI slop in writing is so easy to spot, and I think it means the long-term cybersecurity equilibrium is actually a happy one.

Prompt strategy will converge on the best / most efficient prompts per target (and prompting value will decrease as models get better; prompting has declined precipitously in value already).

Whenever you see the model going away and thinking for 80 minutes, that's prompting becoming less valuable.

tweets.text:
Contributed to this great piece
Here’s your TL;DR ↓

• Study scope: 71,697 buyers; $91.3B gross acquisitions across ten tokenised dollar-yield products; tokenised RWAs up 179% to $33.8B by July 2026.

• No TradFi flow in the attributable pool: of $12.4B we could attribute to buyer types, 66% was protocol/DAO treasuries and zero dollars were traced to pensions, asset managers, or banks.

• Extreme concentration: wallets writing checks ≥ $1M are ~4% of buyers but account for ~93% of acquired notional.

• Largest institutional tickets go to credit-like products: @centrifuge JAAA median institutional cheque ≈ $29.1M, ~3x the next-largest product.

• USDC is the dominant settlement rail: ~80% ($17.4B) of acquisitions used USDC; USDT accounts for ~$4.4B (mostly via syrupUSDT).

• Buyer base is fresh capital: median first activity dates to mid-2024 (retail ≈ Mar‑2024; institutional ≈ Jul‑2024); few wallets predate 2021.

• Primary subscription dominates: 93–100% of acquisitions are primary mints; genuine DEX entry is 0–6% across products except @ethena sUSDe at 55% DEX-sourced.

• Leverage is limited and concentrated: most products lack lending markets; mF-ONE peaked near 42% borrowed vs notional then unwound to ~8%; AA_FalconXUSDC peaked ~29% and was ~25% outstanding on July 7, 2026.

• Retention skews to credit/treasury: TradFi-like credit funds retain ~68% of buyers at one year; tokenised treasuries ~60%; crypto-native carry/credit products churn to ~28% by year-end.

• Operating hours skew non‑US: EMEA ~42%, APAC ~40%, Americas ~18% by buyer activity window.

• Unattributed institutional capital largely CEX/DeFi-funded after two hops: of $5.17B otherwise-unattributed, ~40% traces to CEX funding (Binance ≈ $1.12B noted), ~38% DeFi-native, ~22% unidentified.

• Net composite APY demanded by large allocators rises with credit-likeness: ≈2.3% on synthetic-dollar carry up to ≈7.4% on gated private-credit funds.

• Retail is headcount, not capital: @Ondo USDY is 95.6% retail by buyer count yet those wallets hold under 1% of USDY value; Ethena sUSDe concentrates most retail presence but retail remains a minority of capital.

• Secondary liquidity is the biggest composability gap: without DEX/secondary depth, RWAs remain subscription products and rarely function as continuous collateral or composable assets.

The onchain demand profile looks like freshly formed crypto-native treasuries, exchanges and funds spinning up purpose-built wallets in 2024 and buying primarily at issuance via USDC rails.

Where lending markets and DEX liquidity exist, holders post collateral and looping creates leverage (seen in mF-ONE and AA_FalconX); where markets don’t exist, capital sits in spot earning base yield.

These dynamics explain concentration, the preference for primary issuance, and why retail shows up in counts but not capital share.

tweets.text:
Not exactly for $AAOI (they're working on CPO too, just a little further behind in commercialization).

There's a podcast that featured the $SIVE CEO recently called: "Market Insights: Photonics & AI".

Most interesting take was: 

"Pluggables will be around for the next 10 years".

There's a lot of CPO + RNG/MRC optimization TAM cannablization arguments for pluggables recently, but looks like it's here to stay.

And that there's going to be multiple architecture paths going forward (CEO also NPO alongside CPO as an major opportunity in the next 5 years). 

As for challenges: Vickram stated manufacturing capacity + availability of raw material is the main bottleneck. Likely referencing the existing bottlenecks with upstream CW laser ramp. 

From the podcast, seems like a large focus was on pluggable markets due to larger near-term TAM (which is understandable with $JBL + other players), with CPO being the north star. 

So it might be interesting if $SIVE M&A ambitions were to acquire more pluggable optical transceiver IP, like what $LITE did with Cloud Light.

But TLDR:

Lot of different paths moving forward, pluggable here to stay, CPO seems like North Star for where industry is heading. 

Feels like there's room for every qualified laser supplier given the industry demand is growing faster than what they can all collectively make.

thread_tweets.text:
The most important price in capital markets is the price of money (interest rates). It is the foundation of everything else. Once you start manipulating it, artificially suppressing it, you risk destroying the banks and with them the entire financial system.

The reason it appeared to "work" from 2010 to 2022 was that there was no inflation. Now we have 64 consecutive months of inflation above 2%, and it's currently above 3%. You can't repeat financial repression with inflation.

All attempts at financial repression seem to work only because they are undertaken in a benign environment (with little to no inflation). When that environment turns hostile or inflationary, all financial repression blows up, creating more pain and suffering than it alleviated during the benign period.
---
We know this because it has been tried for thousands of years and it never works.  Humanity makes the same mistakes over and over, always thinking "this time is different." It never is.

The Babylonian rulers in 2400 BC  regularly issued Andurarum (debt jubilees) to reset unpayable debts and cap compounding interest.

The first recorded case of QE/money printing was Roman Emperor Tiberius in 33 AD when he established a state-funded credit facility, zero interest rates, and easing of collateral requirements. And EVERY TIME it has been tried, it ends in disaster and often ruins a civilization. 

John Law was the first literal "money printer" (via the mint) in 1720.

Weimar Germany tried it in the 1920s

In the 1930s, Japan's Finance Minister Takahashi Korekiyo attempted a form of zero-interest rates and money printing (central bank deficit financing), and in 1936, when he attempted to end it, he was assassinated, shot, and his body was mutilated with a samurai sword.

The reason it appeared to "work" from 2010 to 2022 was that there was no inflation. Now we have 64 consecutive months of inflation above 2%, and it's currently above 3%. You can't repeat financial repression with inflation.

All attempts at financial repression seem to work only because they are undertaken in a benign environment (with little to no inflation). When that environment turns hostile or inflationary, all financial repression blows up, creating more pain and suffering than it alleviated during the benign period.

tweets.text:
$INTC up 11% on June earnings. 

• My bottom line. What's going on is much bigger than INTC. Results are a sign that we are still much early in AI than many think.

• I think of Intel CPUs as an accessory to the AI buildout. Its business is getting pulled along by NVDA.

• To put it into perspective, $NVDA’s GPU business is 13x bigger than Intels most exciting segment, DCAI, with NVDA expected to grow at 96% in July. Intel DCAI just printed 59% growth. Intel is more successful at beating the law of large numbers. 

• Intel is great and there are better AI companies to own to invest in AI. Deepwater does not own shares.

• The guide ups have been getting larger over the past two quarters. Assuming they hit the high end of guidance, they guided Sep revenue up 11% this quarter, up 13% in Mar-25, up 1% in Dec-25, and up 3% in Sep-25.

• This is important because it’s a sign analysts are having a hard time keeping up with the true growth. That means the Street still doesn’t get the big picture of what’s going on. What is going on is we are earlier than most believe in AI infrastructure buildout.

• Margins are likely going to be flat in September, at 42%. That’s a good sign vs what we say from GOOG last night.

• NVDA is 13x bigger and almost 2x.Valuation: Intel trades at 61x CY27 EPS. NVDA at 17x, and AMD at 40x.

More to come on the call.

Technically, the deal has not officially collapsed yet. But in reality, it is over. The Prime Minister and the Minister of Defense have already made it clear that the transaction will not be approved for national security reasons.

The fact that a company was willing to pay around $35 per share, with the possibility that other bidders could have offered even more, says a lot about $ZIM ’s value.

When an acquirer is willing to pay that kind of price, it’s usually because they believe the business is worth significantly more than what they are paying, often 50% or more above the purchase price. It gives you an idea of the underlying value they saw in ZIM.

tweets.text:
People can't conceive of how fast this is coming.

Look at just the last few years of AI. It's moved faster than we can legislate, faster than we can debate, faster than any institution was built to handle. And that's the slow part. It only accelerates from here.

That's a huge elephant in the room. Shifts this big would normally go through a democratic process. People debate it, it works its way through… But that takes decades. And we don't have decades.

When the ground moves this fast and process can't keep up, governments reach for whatever knob they have. And control tends to get more centralised, not because anyone wants it, but because the democratic process simply isn't fast enough to catch what's coming.

This is the part of the Economic Singularity nobody wants to look at. It isn't just markets and machines moving to a speed we can't follow. It's our whole system of governing arriving too late.

I sat down with @PeterDiamandis and @salimismail  to go over how the next few years play out. As ever, please enjoy.

tweets.text:
ERC-8004 doesn't work and trustless agents can't be trusted

— According to the first empirical study on ERC-8004

The paper analyzed 170,000+ agent identities & 150,000 records across Ethereum, BSC, and Base

> Only 3-15% of agent identities are valid (the rest are empty)
> It only costs $0.0027–$0.055/feedback to manipulate the reputation
> 73-91% of reviewers activity flagged as Sybils, removing them left 86.8% of agents on Base and 77.9% on BSC with 0 valid reviews

and The MOST interesting part
> When client pays to an agent (x402), the payment can't be linked to the reputation of that agent (ERC-8004)
> Some agents use the same wallet as the other agent, some use ACP escrow contract, both of which make it difficult to attribute the job to the agent that performs the job

This begs the question

If ERC-8004 in its current form can't be used as identity, trust, and reputation layer, what's the alternative?

> 73-91% of reviewers activity flagged as Sybils, removing them left 86.8% of agents on Base and 77.9% on BSC with 0 valid reviews

and The MOST interesting part
> When client pays to an agent (x402), the payment can't be linked to the reputation of that agent (ERC-8004)
> Some agents use the same wallet as the other agent, some use ACP escrow contract, both of which make it difficult to attribute the job to the agent that performs the job

This begs the question

If ERC-8004 in its current form can't be used as identity, trust, and reputation layer, what's the alternative?

The paper also suggests path forward for ERC-8004. Worth a read if you're into agentic commerce.

Honestly, I don't think this changes things much. We just have to test agents out + do better DD on the team that provide the agents.
